content.problemStatement: Your friend Chef has made a cake for you on your birthday. Before having the cake you two have made some cuts on the cake. If you consider the cake as a rectangular grid with co-ordinates from (**0**,**0**) to (**n**,**n**), then each of the cut can be considered as a rectilinear polygon (a polygon with sides parallel to the axis and possibly self-intersecting). These cuts turn the cake into several pieces. Now you two want to distribute the pieces among yourself. Since it is your birthday, your friend has given you a choice of choosing some pieces for yourself. You are hungry and you want to have the whole cake. But you don't want to make your friend suspicious. So instead of having all the pieces you want to select the set of pieces such that no pair of pieces have a common side. You want to maximize the total amount of cakes you can have. This amount can be measured as the sum of the areas of the top surface for each piece (or the amount of cake in each piece is the area of the polygon that define that piece).
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each test case starts with two integers n and m, where n is the size of the cake and m is the number of cuts. Then, for each cut, there are 4 integers x1, y1, x2, y2, representing the coordinates of the two points that define the cut. The input is terminated by a newline.
content.outputFormat: For each test case, output the maximum total area of the cake pieces that can be chosen such that no pair of pieces have a common side.

solution.approach: To solve this problem, we can use a greedy approach. First, we need to find all the pieces of the cake after the cuts. Then, we sort the pieces by their areas in descending order. Finally, we iterate over the sorted pieces and choose the pieces that do not have a common side with any previously chosen piece.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * m * n) — the time complexity is dominated by the iteration over all the pieces of the cake for each test case.
solution.spaceComplexity: O(T * m * n) — the space complexity is dominated by the storage of all the pieces of the cake for each test case.
